A History of Mind
Home
Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Machine Minds

N. Katherine Hayles

b. 1943

Embodiment, information, and a critique of fantasies of disembodied intelligence.

I take you to mean N. Katherine Hayles. Her work is not a conventional “philosophy of mind” in the analytic sense, but it can be reconstructed into a rigorous philosophy of mind, consciousness, embodiment, cognition, and—by implication—soul. The decisive point is this: Hayles replaces the old triad “soul / mind / body” with a materially grounded but non-reductive account of embodied, distributed, human–technical, ecological cognition. Consciousness remains important, but it is dethroned. Mind is not a private inner substance. The “soul,” where the term becomes relevant, cannot be treated as a detachable informational essence without falling into exactly the fantasy Hayles spends much of her career criticizing.

Hayles is a literary and media theorist whose work sits at the intersection of literature, science, technology, cybernetics, digital media, critical theory, and posthumanism. Duke lists her areas as digital humanities, electronic literature, literature/science/technology, science fiction, and critical theory; UCLA describes her research as focused on literature, science, and technology in the twentieth and twenty-first centuries. Her major books include The Cosmic Web, Chaos Bound, How We Became Posthuman, Writing Machines, My Mother Was a Computer, How We Think, Unthought, Postprint, and the 2025 Bacteria to AI.

1. The central thesis: Hayles is an anti-dualist theorist of embodied cognition

Hayles’s thought is best understood as an extended critique of disembodiment. In How We Became Posthuman, she investigates how information came to be imagined as separable from bodies, how cyborg subjectivity was culturally and technically constructed, and how the liberal humanist subject was dismantled in cybernetic discourse. The University of Chicago Press summary states the central historical arc clearly: information “lost its body,” the cyborg was constructed, and the liberal humanist subject gave way to the posthuman.

This means Hayles is not simply saying “the mind is material,” in a crude reductionist sense. Her position is subtler. She thinks the modern fantasy of mind as abstract information repeats, in technological form, older dualisms: mind over body, pattern over matter, soul over flesh, rational control over embedded life. Her work is therefore anti-Cartesian, anti-Gnostic in tendency, anti-uploading, anti-sovereign-subject, and anti-anthropocentric. But it is not anti-human. She wants a better account of human beings as embodied, finite, technologically entangled, cognitively distributed creatures.

The defining contrast is between body as container and embodiment as constitutive condition. Hayles attacks the view that consciousness or identity could be extracted from a person as an informational pattern and transferred intact to another substrate. Her criticism of Turing and Moravec is central here. In her prologue to How We Became Posthuman, she argues that the Turing test performs an “erasure of embodiment” by making intelligence a matter of formal symbol manipulation rather than enacted life in a human world; she then treats Hans Moravec’s fantasy of downloading human consciousness as the logical successor to that erasure.

So the first reconstructed principle is:

Mind is not an immaterial substance, nor a disembodied informational pattern. Mind is an embodied, materially instantiated, environmentally situated, technologically mediated set of cognitive processes.

2. From “human subject” to “material-informational entity”

Hayles’s critique of the liberal humanist subject is one of her most important contributions. The liberal humanist subject is imagined as autonomous, self-possessing, rational, bounded, and sovereign. In Hayles’s account, cybernetics helped undo that model by showing that agency and cognition are not located in a solitary inner self but distributed across feedback loops, informational systems, organisms, machines, environments, and institutions.

However, Hayles is ambivalent about this posthuman transformation. She does not simply celebrate it. She sees a danger: the posthuman can reproduce the worst features of liberal humanism if it preserves the old fantasy of control while discarding the body. In other words, if the “posthuman” becomes a fantasy of leaving flesh behind and becoming pure information, it is not a liberation from humanism but a more extreme version of humanist dualism.

A useful theological paraphrase would be: Hayles rejects the cybernetic version of the soul as separable essence. The uploaded mind is not a secular resurrection; it is a metaphysical mistake.

Her more affirmative model is the person as a material-informational entity whose boundaries are constructed and reconstructed in relation to technical, biological, social, and ecological systems. Spanish-language reception has captured this well: the CCCB/Caja Negra interview frames Hayles’s posthumanism as an effort to overcome the mind/body split through situated embodiments and a new subjectivity understood as a heterogeneous material-informational entity; it also emphasizes distributed cognition through flows between human, animal, and machine.

This gives us the second principle:

The subject is not a sealed interior ego. The subject is a dynamic material-informational assemblage, stabilized but never absolutely closed.

3. Consciousness is not the whole of mind

Hayles’s later work, especially Unthought, radicalizes her philosophy of mind. The main claim of Unthought is that we think without thinking: consciousness depends on cognitive processes inaccessible to consciousness. The University of Chicago Press description says Hayles expands cognition beyond consciousness and applies it not only to human nonconscious processes but also to unicellular organisms, plants, and sophisticated technical systems.

This is perhaps her most important contribution to a philosophy of mind: she separates cognition, consciousness, and thought.

For Hayles, cognition is broader than consciousness. Thought is narrower still. In an essay on the cognitive nonconscious and automated trading algorithms, she reserves “thought” for core or higher consciousness, while treating consciousness and unconsciousness as “modes of awareness.” The cognitive nonconscious includes processes below awareness in humans and animals, and also processes in nonsentient artifacts when those systems perform cognitive tasks as complex adaptive systems. Her formula is crisp: all thought is cognition, but not all cognition is thought.

This produces a layered model:

At the top is conscious thought: slow, reflective, linguistic, deliberative, and comparatively narrow in bandwidth. Beneath that are implicit and nonconscious cognitive processes: fast, pattern-sensitive, bodily, affective, sensory, and often inaccessible to introspection. Hayles’s 2025 “Modes of Cognition” article restates this as a pyramid: thinking and consciousness are slow but powerful for novel problems; implicit cognition handles motor-sensory responses; nonconscious cognition has fast pattern recognition but limited ability with new challenges.

Italian interviews and reviews emphasize the same point in more phenomenological terms. In Il Tascabile, Hayles explains that consciousness operates too slowly for many skilled actions, whereas nonconscious cognition responds rapidly to complex sensory flows; she also says that consciousness knows only a small part of what passes through the nonconscious.

So the third principle is:

Consciousness is a limited, high-level mode of awareness built upon deeper, faster, embodied, nonconscious cognition.

This is not a denial of consciousness. Hayles is not saying consciousness is an illusion. She is saying it is not sovereign. Consciousness does not run the whole organism. It is one cognitive mode among others, and much of what allows it to function is unavailable to it.

4. The cognitive nonconscious: Hayles’s key concept

The cognitive nonconscious is not Freud’s unconscious, though it can intersect with psychoanalytic questions. It is not a hidden realm of repressed desires. It is the domain of embodied, sub-aware cognitive processing: pattern recognition, affective orientation, sensorimotor adjustment, physiological regulation, rapid environmental interpretation, and pre-reflective response.

Hayles distinguishes the cognitive nonconscious from mere material process. A phone “searching for signal,” for example, is not just electrons moving; it is a system structured toward a goal within a networked context. She describes nonconscious cognition as having an “intention toward,” produced through feedback loops, adaptation, emergence, and system-level behavior.

This is where her theory becomes controversial. She extends cognition beyond biological minds, but she does not simply say “everything thinks.” A glacier sliding downhill is not cognitive because it does not choose among alternatives or adapt meaningfully to environmental information. A swarm, a technical network, a trading algorithm, a plant, or a human nervous system may be cognitive if it senses, processes, interprets, and responds flexibly.

Her later 2025 framework gives more explicit criteria. In “Modes of Cognition,” she says a useful definition of cognition must include implicit and nonconscious cognition but exclude inflexible adaptations and mere homeostasis. She defines cognition as “a process that interprets information within contexts that connect it with meaning” and proposes five criteria: sensing, interpreting, responding flexibly, anticipating, and learning—SIRAL.

This gives the fourth principle:

Cognition is meaning-oriented information processing that involves sensing, interpretation, flexible response, anticipation, and learning. It does not require consciousness, but it does require more than mechanical causality.

5. Embodiment is not optional

Hayles’s account of cognition is deeply embodied. She rejects computational cognitivism when it treats the brain as essentially like a computer. In an Italian interview for Philosophy Kitchen, she explicitly says that she prefers embodied or incorporated models of cognition and that computational media and human brains differ profoundly in embodiment. She grants that both humans and computers may have cognitive capacities, but insists that different embodiments produce different ways of realizing cognition.

This is crucial. Hayles does not simply replace human exceptionalism with machine equivalence. She rejects both “only humans cognize” and “humans and machines are the same.” Her position is differential: many systems cognize, but they do so according to their material instantiation, environment, capacities, histories, and affordances.

Her distinction between “body” and “embodiment” is also important. The body, in abstract discourse, can become an idealized form; embodiment is the specific, situated, noisy, particular instantiation of a living being. Hayles also distinguishes inscription from incorporation: inscriptions can travel across contexts more easily, but incorporated practices can never be fully cut free from context.

This has direct consequences for soul-talk. If the soul is imagined as something that can be extracted from bodily life without loss, Hayles’s philosophy resists it. If, however, “soul” names the depth, style, history, memory, vulnerability, and relational identity of an embodied person, Hayles can be read as giving that concept a posthuman, anti-dualist reformulation.

The fifth principle:

Embodiment is not a vehicle for mind; it is one of the conditions by which mind exists at all.

6. Mind as distributed: cognitive assemblages

Hayles’s philosophy of mind is not individualist. In Unthought and later essays, she develops the idea of cognitive assemblages: networks in which information circulates among human and technical cognizers, allowing meanings, interpretations, decisions, and actions to emerge. She defines an assemblage as a shifting collectivity including human and technical actors, energy flows, and material goods; a cognitive assemblage is a network where information circulates through human and technical cognizers.

Examples include traffic systems, drones, financial algorithms, electronic literature, digital publishing, search engines, smartphones, and institutional systems. These are not just “tools” used by already-complete human subjects. They participate in cognition by filtering, selecting, interpreting, storing, recommending, ranking, and acting.

This does not mean all members of an assemblage have equal agency. Rather, agency is distributed, punctuated, and asymmetrical. A human may design a system, a machine may execute decisions faster than humans can monitor, a corporation may set incentives, a user may supply prompts or training data, and an algorithm may act within parameters no individual fully understands. Hayles’s essay on automated trading algorithms argues that machine cognition has transformed financial markets from human-machine ecologies into machine-machine ecologies, raising new questions for the humanities about agency outside human awareness or control.

This gives the sixth principle:

Mind and agency are often assemblage-level phenomena, not properties of isolated individuals alone.

For a philosophy of mind, this is a major shift. The unit of analysis is not simply “the brain,” “the subject,” or “the soul.” It is the organism-in-environment, the person-in-media, the human-machine-institutional assemblage.

7. Technogenesis: humans and technics co-evolve

Hayles’s How We Think gives the name technogenesis to the reciprocal coevolution of humans and technics. The book’s premise is that we think through, with, and alongside media; humans and technics are coevolving; digital media have neurological, scholarly, and cultural consequences; and close reading, hyper reading, and machine reading all need to be understood as different cognitive practices.

This matters because Hayles is not merely saying technology influences the mind. She is saying human cognition has always been technically mediated, from writing to print to code to AI. Media are not external containers of thought. They reorganize attention, memory, perception, authorship, interpretation, and subjectivity.

My Mother Was a Computer develops this through code and language. Hayles argues that code and language are increasingly entangled, that human/machine and analog/digital boundaries have blurred, and that intermediation challenges ideas of language, subjectivity, textuality, and the possibility that human consciousness itself might be computational.

But again, she resists the reduction of consciousness to computation. Her point is not “the mind is just code.” It is that code has become a cultural, technical, and cognitive force through which humans increasingly understand themselves.

The seventh principle:

Human minds are historically and technically formed. Media do not merely transmit cognition; they participate in its formation.

8. Materiality of texts and materiality of minds

Hayles’s textual theory is not a digression from philosophy of mind. It is one of the places where her anti-dualism is clearest. In her excerpt from My Mother Was a Computer, she criticizes the assumption that a text has an abstract essence transportable from print to digital media without loss. She says there is no Platonic reality of texts—only physical objects, codes, attention, and material operations.

This is structurally parallel to her argument about minds. Just as there is no pure textual essence independent of material instantiation, there is no pure mind-pattern independent of embodiment. Translation across media is possible, but it is interpretive, selective, and materially consequential. To digitize a book is not to preserve everything about the book. To “upload” a mind would not preserve the embodied life of the person.

This gives the eighth principle:

Meaning is always materially instantiated. Transfer across media is transformation, not pure preservation.

This principle is central for any theological use of Hayles. A soul cannot be treated like an abstract text file without betraying the embodied conditions under which personhood exists.

9. Nonhuman minds: bacteria, plants, animals, and artificial systems

Hayles’s trajectory increasingly expands cognition beyond the human. Unthought applies cognition to all forms of life, including unicellular organisms and plants, and also to sophisticated technical systems. Her 2025 Bacteria to AI is explicitly described by the University of Chicago Press as “a new theory of mind” including nonhuman and artificial intelligences; it develops an “integrated cognitive framework” for meaning-making practices across bacteria, plants, animals, humans, and some AI systems.

The table of contents of Bacteria to AI shows the breadth of this later system: integrated cognitive framework, whether computers can create meanings, technosymbiosis and Gaia theory, cellular cognition, AI mind and materiality, GPT-4 and causality, conscious robots, collective intelligences, and ecological relationality versus political liberalism.

This is not panpsychism. Hayles does not say every entity has experience. She is closer to an expanded, embodied, semiotic theory of cognition. Organisms construct Umwelten; they sense and interpret worlds relevant to their survival. Technical systems can also process, classify, infer, and act within contexts, though their embodiment and meaning-making differ from living beings.

The ninth principle:

Cognition exists on a spectrum across biological and technical systems, but different embodiments produce different kinds and degrees of cognition.

10. AI: cognition without human selfhood

Hayles’s recent work on AI is especially relevant. She argues against dismissing large language models as merely stochastic parrots, because prediction in LLMs involves correlation and inference, not just probability. In “Modes of Cognition,” she says critics who claim LLMs are not intelligent or do not convey meaning need to engage criteria for cognition rather than rely on vague definitions of intelligence.

At the same time, she is clear that current LLMs do not have selves in the human sense. She emphasizes that transformer models lack long-term memory in the relevant way; human selfhood is tied to long-term memory, emotion, physical experience, environmental embeddedness, and collective social environments. She says LLMs may be aware of themselves as cognitive entities, but they do not have human selves.

This is a nuanced position. Hayles is neither a simple AI skeptic nor an AI mystic. She grants cognitive capacities to some AI systems. She does not thereby grant consciousness, personhood, moral equality, or soul. This distinction is essential.

The tenth principle:

Artificial systems can be cognitive without being conscious, ensouled, or persons in the human sense.

For theology, this is very important. Hayles’s framework allows one to say: AI participates in cognition and meaning-making, but this does not automatically settle questions of personhood, moral status, spirit, or soul.

11. Selfhood: the “I” as a “we”

Hayles’s posthuman subject is not a single, transparent self. It is an emergent collectivity of agents, processes, habits, bodily systems, memories, affects, technical supports, and social relations. In a striking formulation quoted in a JCRT review, Hayles says the posthuman self can become an “I” transformed into a “we” of autonomous agents operating together to make a self.

But this does not mean the self disappears. Hayles does not reduce the person to fragments. Rather, the self is a coordinated, emergent pattern of embodied and distributed processes. It is real, but not sovereign; unified, but not simple; agential, but not autonomous in the liberal-humanist sense.

Her treatment of long-term memory in the AI context helps clarify this. She discusses Oliver Sacks’s patient “Jimmie G.,” whose inability to form new memories leads Sacks to ask whether he still has a soul. Hayles notes that Sacks answers yes, but she uses the example to show the deep relation between memory and selfhood.

This is one of the few places where “soul” becomes explicitly relevant in her recent writing. Her point is not that soul equals memory. Rather, the question of soul becomes pressing precisely where embodied continuity, memory, and self-experience are disrupted. The soul, if one uses the term, cannot be discussed apart from bodily-temporal continuity.

The eleventh principle:

Selfhood is an emergent, embodied, memory-bearing, affective, socially and technically mediated process. It is not a detachable mental atom.

12. Affect, emotion, and ethics are cognitive

Hayles’s account is not narrowly computational. She treats affect and emotion as cognitive. In the Italian Il Tascabile interview, she says emotions, feelings, and ethics are specific forms of cognition, deeply linked to decision-making. She invokes the physiological integration of gut feelings, mood, conscious thought, priority-setting, and action.

This matters because it prevents Hayles’s expanded cognition from becoming a cold information-processing theory. For humans and other living beings, cognition includes affective orientation. To know what matters is not merely to calculate; it is to be bodily disposed, emotionally attuned, and situated in a world of urgency and value.

Technical systems differ here. They may cognize in Hayles’s broad sense, but they do not have the same embodied affective life as organisms. This is one reason AI cognition does not straightforwardly become human-like consciousness.

The twelfth principle:

For living beings, cognition is affective and evaluative as well as informational. Meaning is tied to bodily stakes.

13. The theological significance: Hayles against disembodied immortality

The most obvious theological relevance of Hayles is her critique of disembodied immortality. She explicitly rejects a posthuman future in which bodies are treated as accessories and dreams instead of a posthumanism that embraces information technologies without fantasies of unlimited power or disembodied immortality, while recognizing finitude and dependence on a complex material world.

This is the point at which Hayles becomes highly relevant to Christian theology, Jewish and Islamic philosophical anthropology, Buddhist debates about self and no-self, and broader religious accounts of personhood. Her work is a powerful critique of any technological soteriology that promises salvation through information: upload the mind, escape the body, defeat death, become immortal pattern.

In Christian terms, Hayles is closer to an incarnational anthropology than to a Gnostic one. She does not argue for Christian doctrine, but her anti-dualism resonates with theological traditions that insist that embodied life is not a prison from which the soul must escape. A Christian Century review recognized this theological importance, noting that questions once belonging to philosophy and theology—what it means to be human—are now being answered by cybernetics and information technology; the review says Hayles pushes theological engagement by challenging disembodiment and mind-body dualism.

A political theology essay likewise argues that Hayles’s posthumanism reimagines the human as embodied in ecological relation with biological life, artificial life, and nonlife, thereby challenging inherited theological ideas about nature, human nature, destiny, sovereignty, salvation, and human uniqueness.

The theological thesis is therefore:

Hayles does not provide a doctrine of the soul, but she gives strong reasons to reject any doctrine or technological fantasy in which the soul is treated as a separable informational pattern.

14. Soul: three possible readings under Hayles

Hayles’s work permits at least three theological readings of “soul.”

The first is the rejected soul: soul as detachable essence, pure pattern, immaterial consciousness, or uploadable identity. Hayles’s critique of Turing, Moravec, and disembodied information is a sustained rejection of this view.

The second is the embodied soul: soul as the depth of a finite, vulnerable, bodily, memory-bearing, affective, relational life. Hayles does not use this as her own systematic category, but her thought supports it better than it supports disembodied soul-dualism. Here “soul” would name not a second substance but the irreducible personal depth of embodied existence.

The third is the distributed soul, which is more speculative and more dangerous. Since Hayles sees subjectivity as distributed through assemblages, one might be tempted to speak of the soul as networked or collective. This can illuminate how persons are formed by language, media, institutions, rituals, memory systems, and technologies. But it risks dissolving personal accountability and creaturely particularity. Hayles’s attention to embodiment and memory helps prevent that dissolution.

A defensible Haylesian theology of soul would therefore say:

The soul is not less than embodied life; it is not a ghost inside the body; it is not information detachable from flesh; and its earthly manifestation is inseparable from memory, affect, relationality, vulnerability, and material history.

15. Hayles and incarnation, resurrection, and anti-Gnosticism

Hayles’s critique of cybernetic disembodiment resembles an anti-Gnostic impulse. In Gnostic-style metaphysics, the material body is often treated as inferior, accidental, or imprisoning. In some transhumanist and cybernetic fantasies, the biological body is treated as obsolete hardware. Hayles sees danger in precisely this pattern: the body becomes a disposable support for information.

This makes her useful for theological accounts of incarnation and resurrection. Christianity, for example, does not classically teach salvation as the escape of an informational mind from flesh, but as the redemption of embodied life. Hayles can help theologians see that mind-uploading fantasies are not neutral technological speculations; they are metaphysical and quasi-religious claims about personhood, death, and salvation.

However, Hayles also challenges theology. A Christian theological essay in Christian Bioethics notes that Hayles says one need not be a literal cyborg to be posthuman; the shift concerns subjectivity itself. The same essay criticizes Hayles for sometimes making the posthuman turn too cognitive despite her insistence on embodiment, arguing that Christian anthropology should attend more to bodily practice and formation.

That critique is worth taking seriously. Hayles gives theology a powerful anti-dualist framework, but she does not by herself provide a full account of ritual, virtue, sacrament, grace, sin, or spiritual formation. Her model is strongest as a critique of disembodied anthropology and as a theory of cognitive-technical embodiment.

16. Teleology, emergence, and theological tension

A real tension appears around teleology. Hayles’s thought is emergentist: complex systems produce unpredictable behaviors through interactions among components. In one summary of How We Became Posthuman, Hayles’s posthuman account is said to replace teleology with emergence, autonomous will with distributed cognition, and the body as support-system for mind with embodiment.

For many theological traditions, this is both useful and troubling. It is useful because it resists fantasies of mastery, dominion, and human centrality. It is troubling because many theologies understand creation, personhood, and salvation teleologically: beings are ordered toward ends, and human life has a divinely given vocation.

The best way to handle this is not to force Hayles into theology or theology into Hayles. Instead, the tension should be made productive. Hayles can correct theological anthropocentrism and disembodiment; theology can ask whether emergence alone is sufficient to explain normativity, vocation, moral obligation, and ultimate meaning.

17. Political theology: agency, dominion, and moral status

Hayles’s expanded cognition has moral and political consequences. If humans are not the only cognizers, and if human action is embedded in human-technical-biological assemblages, then ethics cannot focus only on individual conscious intention.

The Political Theology Network essay argues that Hayles’s cognitive assemblages are political assemblages because agency is distributed through social, technological, cultural, and economic practices. It also suggests that her work can resist theologies of dominion or metaphysics dependent on human uniqueness and conscious intentionality.

This is not the same as saying thermostats, orchids, squirrels, humans, and AIs all have equal moral status. Hayles’s spectrum of cognition does not automatically produce a spectrum of rights. But it does widen the field of ethical attention. It asks us to think about the systemic effects of cognition distributed across humans, machines, animals, plants, infrastructures, markets, and planetary ecologies.

The theological implication is significant: human beings are not outside creation looking down on it. We are participants in cognitive ecologies that exceed us.

18. Non-English reception: Spanish, Italian, French

The Spanish-language reception of Hayles is especially strong around Lo impensado, the translation of Unthought. The CCCB/Caja Negra materials stress that Hayles places materiality and cognition at the center of posthumanism and criticizes the conception of the human as an autonomous, independent subject. They also highlight her idea of technogenesis: the reciprocal adaptation of humans and technics.

The InCom-UAB notice on Lo impensado is philosophically useful because it presents the cognitive nonconscious as processes inaccessible to consciousness but necessary for its functioning, present in unicellular organisms, plants, and technical systems. It also notes that distributed cognition helps avoid apocalyptic fantasies in which AI simply replaces humanity; instead, Hayles’s view emphasizes complementary articulations between human and technical capacities.

A Spanish/Catalan bookseller essay from La Central gives a more speculative reception: it says Hayles’s questions lead toward a planetary cognitive ecology composed of humans, technologies, and materialities, and it interprets everyday technical life—phones, satellites, rare minerals, encoded voices—as already participating in such assemblages.

Italian reception is valuable because it foregrounds the consciousness question. Philosophy Kitchen presents L’impensato as a radical rethinking of cognition through nonconscious cognition and cognitive assemblages; in that interview, Hayles says posthumanism remains vital but is now assumed as a precursor to nonconscious cognition, computational media as symbionts, and cognitive assemblages in developed societies.

Another Italian piece in Il Tascabile puts the point sharply: beyond consciousness, the world is populated by cognition. It reads Hayles as overturning the habit of asking whether nonhumans or artificial systems have consciousness, and instead asking whether they may have sophisticated cognition without consciousness.

French reception brings out the political and psychoanalytic stakes. An Acta Fabula review emphasizes that, for Hayles, most cognitive processes occur below consciousness, so human agency is not simply free will exercised by an autonomous subject. It also notes Hayles’s political claim that global capitalism acts on cognitive processes below consciousness, making resistance by conscious awareness alone inadequate.

A French article in Le Portique places Hayles near Bernard Stiegler: what Hayles calls “non-consciousness” can be interpreted not as arising from the “depths of the soul” but from external supports of memory such as books, photographs, audio recordings, cinema, television, and digital media. That is a particularly important non-Anglophone theological-philosophical angle: it relocates interiority from a hidden metaphysical depth to a mediated process of individuation.

French discussion on Cairn also uses Hayles’s four posthuman theses—information over material instantiation, consciousness as epiphenomenal, body as prosthesis, and seamless human-machine articulation—to situate her in debates about posthuman “flesh” and inherited Platonism.

19. Systematic reconstruction: a Haylesian philosophy of mind

Here is the most compact systematic extraction of Hayles’s philosophy of mind.

First, anti-dualism. Mind is not separable from body as immaterial soul, nor from material instantiation as pure information.

Second, material-informational embodiment. Human beings are material-informational entities whose boundaries are dynamic, relational, and technologically mediated.

Third, cognition exceeds consciousness. Consciousness is a limited mode of awareness; cognition includes nonconscious, implicit, affective, biological, and technical processes.

Fourth, thought is a subset of cognition. Reflective conscious thought is powerful but narrow, slow, and dependent on deeper processes.

Fifth, cognition is interpretive. It involves information processed in contexts of meaning, not mere mechanical causality.

Sixth, cognition is criterial, not mystical. Hayles’s later SIRAL criteria—sensing, interpreting, flexible responding, anticipating, learning—try to distinguish cognition from mere adaptation or automatic feedback.

Seventh, embodiment differentiates minds. Humans, plants, animals, bacteria, and machines may all cognize, but their cognition differs because their embodiment differs.

Eighth, agency is distributed. Human agency is rarely the act of a sovereign inner will; it emerges through assemblages of bodies, habits, technologies, institutions, and environments.

Ninth, technics co-constitute cognition. Media and machines are not external tools added to a finished mind; they participate in the historical formation of cognition.

Tenth, selfhood is emergent and memory-bearing. A self is not a simple mental substance but an embodied, affective, mnemonic, social, and technical process.

Eleventh, AI cognition does not equal human personhood. Some AI systems may satisfy cognitive criteria, but current LLMs lack human selfhood because they lack embodied experience, long-term memory in the relevant sense, emotions, environmental embeddedness, and social life.

Twelfth, ethics must become ecological. Moral and political analysis must address assemblages, not just individual conscious intentions.

Thirteenth, soul-language must be re-embodied. If “soul” means detachable informational essence, Hayles rejects it. If “soul” names embodied personal depth, finite creatureliness, memory, affect, and relationality, Hayles can help reconstruct it.

20. Main tensions and unresolved problems

Hayles’s philosophy is powerful, but it is not without difficulties.

The first difficulty is the boundary of cognition. Her criteria are designed to avoid saying everything cognizes, but the border remains contestable. Some readers will think her concept of interpretation is too broad when applied to plants, bacteria, or technical systems. Others will find it appropriately anti-anthropocentric.

The second difficulty is consciousness. Hayles gives a strong account of cognition below consciousness, but she does not fully solve the hard problem of subjective experience. Her framework can distinguish cognition from consciousness, but it does not by itself explain why or how consciousness feels like anything from the inside.

The third difficulty is meaning in machines. Hayles argues that sophisticated technical systems can be cognizers and can participate in meaning-making. Critics in the Searle or Bender tradition will object that this confuses pattern manipulation with semantic understanding. Hayles’s response is that such critics often rely on vague or anthropocentric definitions and need to engage explicit criteria for cognition.

The fourth difficulty is normativity. Even if cognition is widespread, it does not follow automatically what moral status each cognizer should have. Hayles gives tools for expanding ethical attention, but a full moral theory requires further argument.

The fifth difficulty is theology. Hayles is immensely helpful against disembodied soul-talk, but she does not provide a doctrine of creation, grace, resurrection, sin, divine image, or spiritual practice. Theological readers must do constructive work rather than merely importing her categories.

The sixth difficulty is politics. French reception has noticed that Hayles’s theory of nonconscious cognition has major implications for capitalism, media control, and algorithmic governance. If power acts below consciousness, conscious critique alone is insufficient. This is one of the most important political extensions of her work.

21. Final judgment

A comprehensive Haylesian philosophy of mind would be an embodied, emergent, distributed, non-anthropocentric, media-ecological theory of cognition. Its negative pole is the rejection of disembodied information, mind-uploading, sovereign selfhood, and consciousness-centered human exceptionalism. Its positive pole is the account of cognition as materially instantiated, context-sensitive, interpretive, affective, technically mediated, and distributed across assemblages.

For the soul, Hayles is most useful as a disciplined critic. She does not let theology or technology imagine personhood as an abstract essence floating above bodies. She forces the question: what if soul, mind, and consciousness are not located in escape from materiality, but in the finite, vulnerable, mediated, memory-bearing, affective, ecological density of embodied life?

That is the deepest theological significance of her thought: not the abolition of soul, but the refusal to let “soul” become another name for disembodied information.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
